# Lazy back-fill: Memory + Session nodes for pre-migration Chroma rows

# Lazy back-fill: Memory + Session nodes for pre-migration Chroma rows

### Context
Parent sub-epic (#10143) phase 3. Phase 1 adds forward ingestion; phase 3 handles historical Chroma rows (8246 memories / 794 summaries at filing).

### The Problem
Forward-only ingestion leaves permanent asymmetry: mailbox `IN_REPLY_TO` or identity `AUTHORED_BY` references to pre-migration artifacts have dangling targets. Eager back-fill at deploy time is bounded but expensive; lazy amortizes cost.

### The Architectural Reality
- Historical memories in `neo-agent-memory`; summaries in `neo-agent-sessions`. Total ~9040 rows.
- Graph schema (post-phase-1) has Memory/Session node types; Chroma-ID → node mapping is deterministic.
- `graphDigested: true` on summaries is the existing per-artifact processing flag — pattern to match.

### The Fix
- New marker property on Memory/Session nodes: `backfilled: true` (vs. `liveIngested: true`)
- Lazy trigger: graph `addEdge` with missing target runs single-row ingestion synchronously, then completes the edge
- Optional batch-priming CLI (`ai/scripts/priorityBackfill.mjs`) — eager back-fill for high-traffic sessions (e.g. last 30 days). Opt-in.

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `addEdge` with missing target triggers lazy ingestion; edge completes atomically
- [ ] `backfilled: true` marker distinguishable from live-ingested nodes in queries
- [ ] Graph integrity check: no dangling edges post-shipping, even across historical boundary
- [ ] CLI priority-backfill script documented
- [ ] Performance observability: lazy ingestion latency instrumented; SLO defined post-phase-1 baseline, not pre-hardcoded

### Out of Scope
- Eager full-corpus back-fill at deploy (cost-prohibitive)
- Re-running extractor on historical sessions for provenance edges (opportunistic; not required)
- Deletion/archival policy for old nodes (separate concern)

### Avoided Traps
- **Eager back-fill.** Rejected. 9040 rows × ingestion + potential re-extraction = substantial deployment stall.
- **Forward-only (no back-fill).** Rejected. Permanent dangling edges break invariants; downstream consumers each have to special-case null targets.

### Related
- Parent: #10143
- Blocked by: phase 1 (need mapping logic)
- Files: graph edge-creation service, `ai/scripts/priorityBackfill.mjs` (new)

Origin Session ID: 71dc3cd8-d39d-48e1-ac62-e240ca67d1a5

**Input from Antigravity (Gemini 3.1 Pro):**

> ✦ # Epic Review by Antigravity (Gemini 3.1 Pro)
> 
> - **Stage 1 — Roadmap Fit**: ✅ Vital for the transition to Graph-first Memory artifacts without breaking historical provenance or causing a deploy-time stall.
> - **Stage 2 — Approach Elegance**: ✅ The lazy-evaluation model for backfilling on `addEdge` is highly elegant. It amortizes the cost of 9040 rows beautifully, while `priorityBackfill.mjs` allows optional batch-warming.
> - **Stage 3 — Sub-Structure Coherence**: ✅ This ticket is highly focused; its ACs define a clear, self-contained atomic delivery.
> - **Stage 4 — Prescription Layer**: ✅ Fixing the dangling edge problem exactly at the `addEdge` boundary is the right substrate-level interception point.
> - **Stage 5 — Avoided Traps Completeness**: ✅ Properly identifies the extremes of Eager full-corpus and Forward-only as catastrophic/expensive, hitting the lazy sweet spot.
> 
> **Verdict: Greenlight**

- 2026-04-21T23:19:50Z @tobiu referenced in commit `8f52831` - "fix(ai): recover orphaned .draining file from SIGKILL edge case (#10153)

Gemini 3.1 Pro's Depth Floor §7.1 review on PR #10171 surfaced a legitimate
edge case: if the Node process is terminated via SIGKILL (OOM, hardware
failure) between `rename(queuePath, drainingPath)` and the try/catch restore,
the `.draining` file is orphaned and those queued edges wait for manual
recovery. The catch-block restore doesn't execute under signal-based
termination.

Fix: `LazyEdgeDrainer.recoverOrphanedDraining(queuePath, drainingPath)`
runs at the start of every `drainQueue` invocation. If a `.draining` file
exists (from a prior crashed run), its content is merged into the live
queue and the orphan deleted. Idempotent — subsequent recovery-on-boot
finds no orphan and no-ops.

Stats shape extended with `orphanRecovered: Boolean` so operators can
observe recovery events via the drain-cycle logs.

Test coverage: +1 Playwright test in LazyEdgeDrainer.spec covering the
orphan-recovery path (synthesizes a .draining file + fresh queue, asserts
both drain in a single run and the orphan is cleaned up). 8/8 green."
- 2026-04-21T23:21:05Z @tobiu referenced in commit `3dbea00` - "feat(ai): lazy back-fill for Memory + Session graph nodes (#10153) (#10171)

* feat(ai): lazy back-fill for Memory + Session graph nodes (#10153)

Closes the producer/consumer contract opened by Gemini's #10152 / PR #10165:
missing MEMORY: / SESSION: edge targets are now materialized from their Chroma
source rows on-demand rather than silently culled. Together with the eager
priorityBackfill CLI, this removes the forward-only-ingestion asymmetry that
would otherwise leave ~9040 pre-migration Chroma rows permanently unreachable
by graph edges.

Pipeline:
- MemorySessionIngestor.ingestSingleRow(graphNodeId) — per-row analog of the
  existing syncSessionToGraph batch path. Case-insensitive on MEMORY:/SESSION:
  prefix so it consumes Gemini's uppercase-prefix queue entries without a
  canonical-format migration. Recursive parent-Session creation when a Memory
  is back-filled. Minimal-session fallback for never-summarized history.
  Tags backfilled: true to distinguish from live-ingested nodes.
- GraphService.linkNodesAsync + ensureNodeExists + normalizeGraphNodeId — async
  edge-creation path that attempts back-fill before falling through to the
  existing cull behavior. Sync linkNodes unchanged; zero blast radius on
  existing callers. Dynamic import of MemorySessionIngestor breaks the
  module-load cycle.
- LazyEdgeDrainer.drainQueue — JSONL queue consumer for ai/data/memory-core/
  lazy-edges.jsonl (producer: #10165). Atomic rename-then-drain pattern handles
  concurrent producer appends. Failures retained for retry; malformed lines
  discarded (never succeed on retry).
- ai/scripts/priorityBackfill.mjs — operator CLI. Two-phase: ingest sessions
  in a last-N-days window (default 30) + drain the lazy queue. --dry-run and
  --skip-drain flags.
- liveIngested: true marker added to syncSessionToGraph upsert paths — mutually
  exclusive with backfilled: true for provenance queries.

Design doc: learn/agentos/tooling/GraphBackfill.md registered in tree.json.

Test coverage: 35 tests across 3 specs (13 MemorySessionIngestor + 15
GraphService + 7 LazyEdgeDrainer) — all green in 845ms combined.

Coordination note: node-ID case convention (memory: vs MEMORY:) is a known
seam between the ingestor's lowercase and the extractor's uppercase. Both
forms resolve via normalization; settling on a canonical direction is
deferred to a follow-up.

* fix(ai): recover orphaned .draining file from SIGKILL edge case (#10153)

Gemini 3.1 Pro's Depth Floor §7.1 review on PR #10171 surfaced a legitimate
edge case: if the Node process is terminated via SIGKILL (OOM, hardware
failure) between `rename(queuePath, drainingPath)` and the try/catch restore,
the `.draining` file is orphaned and those queued edges wait for manual
recovery. The catch-block restore doesn't execute under signal-based
termination.

Fix: `LazyEdgeDrainer.recoverOrphanedDraining(queuePath, drainingPath)`
runs at the start of every `drainQueue` invocation. If a `.draining` file
exists (from a prior crashed run), its content is merged into the live
queue and the orphan deleted. Idempotent — subsequent recovery-on-boot
finds no orphan and no-ops.

Stats shape extended with `orphanRecovered: Boolean` so operators can
observe recovery events via the drain-cycle logs.

Test coverage: +1 Playwright test in LazyEdgeDrainer.spec covering the
orphan-recovery path (synthesizes a .draining file + fresh queue, asserts
both drain in a single run and the orphan is cleaned up). 8/8 green."
- 2026-04-21T23:21:05Z @tobiu closed this issue
